title: Persistent 429s during scheduled background snapshot restore after tenant credit transfer
preview:
Issue summary: Nimbus Analytics reports repeated 429 responses when their background snapshot restore job runs following a manual tenant credit transfer. This started after a maintenance credit reallocation we performed for the customer on 2026-03-13 02:10 UTC.

Impact: Several thousand restore requests (internal background workers) are being rejected with 429s over a ~20 minute window, causing the restore to stall and retry storms. Customer reports delayed data availability and increased worker churn. No user-facing chat endpoints were affected, but background ingest/restore pipeline throughput dropped by ~80%.

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated tenant lease), region: prod-us-west primary with failover to eu-west. Job runs as scheduled restore via Nimbus orchestration (internal cron) that issues parallel multipart requests to our ingest route.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Perform a tenant credit transfer (billing credit refill / manual top-up) for a dedicated tenant.
2) Trigger background snapshot restore orchestrator that fans out ~200 parallel HTTP PUT requests to /v1/restore/chunk.
3) Observe 429 responses from the ingress layer with headers: Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset.

Observed behavior:
- Majority of API responses return HTTP 429 for the route /v1/restore/chunk for ~18-25 minutes after the credit transfer.
- Throttle headers show "X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0" and intermittent "Retry-After: 30".
- Console shows tenant token bucket refill event at 2026-03-13T02:10:12Z followed by a surge of background restore traffic at 02:11:05Z.
- Billing metrics show a credit transfer completed at 02:10:09Z, but quota refresh on some edge proxies lags by 30-90s.

Expected behavior:
- After credit transfer, tenant quota should be available immediately or within a short bounded delay for background jobs; background restores should not be serially rejected causing repeated retries.

Logs / key metrics / evidence:
- Ingress logs (edge-usw-4): 02:11:06Z -> 02:29:50Z multiple 429 entries, sample: "[02:11:23Z] tenant=nimbus-233 request_id=abc123 path=/v1/restore/chunk status=429 rr=30".
- Rate-limiter traces show token-bucket state: pre-transfer tokens=0, transfer event at 02:10:09Z increased quota, but several edge replicas did not apply new quota until 02:11:45Z due to control-plane cache propagation backoff.
- Serving runtime metrics show no GPU saturation; CPU and memory within normal thresholds.
- Console snapshot: tenant quota balance updated, but route-level route-weight still zeroed out for background lanes for two edge clusters.

Investigation notes (support / SRE):
- Hypothesis A: Credit transfer triggers a partial control-plane update which some edge proxies miss; those proxies continue to enforce old zero-balance token buckets leading to 429s for requests routed to those proxies.
- Hypothesis B: The scheduled restore's very high parallelism combined with per-connection rate windowing causes burst credits to be consumed quickly and refill smoothing prevents immediate recovery.
- Evidence supports both: logs show both delayed quota propagation and sawtooth refill behavior for edge-usw-2 and edge-usw-4.
- Related to rate-limiter code path changed in PR #412 which adjusted caching TTL for tenant refills (see related_github_prs).

Workaround provided to customer:
- Temporary mitigation: reduce restore parallelism to <= 20 concurrent workers and enable exponential backoff with randomized jitter on 429s. This allowed the restore to complete slowly without triggering the cross-proxy 429 wave.
- We also provided a script to pin requests to a single ingress pool (ingress-usw-primary) to avoid hitting lagging replicas while we investigate.

Immediate next steps:
- SRE to validate control-plane propagation: run consistency check across edge replicas for tenant=nimbus-233 and gather last-applied refills. (Assigned to Ben Carter)
- Roll back short TTL caching change from PR #412 on a canary set to observe if propagation lag goes away. (If rollback not feasible, patch to force propagation post-transfer.)
- Ask Billing to schedule credit-transfer events with a small pre-warm ping that forces a forced propagation to all edge replicas.
- Add a short-term console flag to show per-edge token-bucket last-refresh time for rapid troubleshooting.

Notes: Customer is on a dedicated contract and this incident met P1 thresholds due to operational impact on their pipeline. We have preserved request traces and logs in /support/artifacts/SUP-358201 for 30d.
text:
Issue summary: Nimbus Analytics reports repeated 429 responses when their background snapshot restore job runs following a manual tenant credit transfer. This started after a maintenance credit reallocation we performed for the customer on 2026-03-13 02:10 UTC.

Impact: Several thousand restore requests (internal background workers) are being rejected with 429s over a ~20 minute window, causing the restore to stall and retry storms. Customer reports delayed data availability and increased worker churn. No user-facing chat endpoints were affected, but background ingest/restore pipeline throughput dropped by ~80%.

Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated tenant lease), region: prod-us-west primary with failover to eu-west. Job runs as scheduled restore via Nimbus orchestration (internal cron) that issues parallel multipart requests to our ingest route.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Perform a tenant credit transfer (billing credit refill / manual top-up) for a dedicated tenant.
2) Trigger background snapshot restore orchestrator that fans out ~200 parallel HTTP PUT requests to /v1/restore/chunk.
3) Observe 429 responses from the ingress layer with headers: Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset.

- Throttle headers show "X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0" and intermittent "Retry-After: 30".
- Console shows tenant token bucket refill event at 2026-03-13T02:10:12Z followed by a surge of background restore traffic at 02:11:05Z.
- Billing metrics show a credit transfer completed at 02:10:09Z, but quota refresh on some edge proxies lags by 30-90s.

Expected behavior:
- After credit transfer, tenant quota should be available immediately or within a short bounded delay for background jobs; background restores should not be serially rejected causing repeated retries.

Logs / key metrics / evidence:
- Ingress logs (edge-usw-4): 02:11:06Z -> 02:29:50Z multiple 429 entries, sample: "[02:11:23Z] tenant=nimbus-233 request_id=abc123 path=/v1/restore/chunk status=429 rr=30".
- Rate-limiter traces show token-bucket state: pre-transfer tokens=0, transfer event at 02:10:09Z increased quota, but several edge replicas did not apply new quota until 02:11:45Z due to control-plane cache propagation backoff.
- Serving runtime metrics show no GPU saturation; CPU and memory within normal thresholds.
- Console snapshot: tenant quota balance updated, but route-level route-weight still zeroed out for background lanes for two edge clusters.

Investigation notes (support / SRE):
- Hypothesis A: Credit transfer triggers a partial control-plane update which some edge proxies miss; those proxies continue to enforce old zero-balance token buckets leading to 429s for requests routed to those proxies.
- Hypothesis B: The scheduled restore's very high parallelism combined with per-connection rate windowing causes burst credits to be consumed quickly and refill smoothing prevents immediate recovery.
- Evidence supports both: logs show both delayed quota propagation and sawtooth refill behavior for edge-usw-2 and edge-usw-4.
- Related to rate-limiter code path changed in PR #412 which adjusted caching TTL for tenant refills (see related_github_prs).

Workaround provided to customer:

- We also provided a script to pin requests to a single ingress pool (ingress-usw-primary) to avoid hitting lagging replicas while we investigate.

Immediate next steps:
- SRE to validate control-plane propagation: run consistency check across edge replicas for tenant=nimbus-233 and gather last-applied refills. (Assigned to Ben Carter)
- Roll back short TTL caching change from PR #412 on a canary set to observe if propagation lag goes away. (If rollback not feasible, patch to force propagation post-transfer.)
- Ask Billing to schedule credit-transfer events with a small pre-warm ping that forces a forced propagation to all edge replicas.
- Add a short-term console flag to show per-edge token-bucket last-refresh time for rapid troubleshooting.

Notes: Customer is on a dedicated contract and this incident met P1 thresholds due to operational impact on their pipeline. We have preserved request traces and logs in /support/artifacts/SUP-358201 for 30d.

title: Go SDK: streaming chat reader blocks indefinitely when server closes connection
preview: Customer reports Go SDK streaming chat occasionally never returns from Next() after the server has closed the TCP connection. They suspect the SDK does not notice EOF promptly. Observed on go1.22, redwood-sdk-go v0.9.1. Impact: goroutines leak and request cancellation fails.
text: Customer reports Go SDK streaming chat occasionally never returns from Next() after the server has closed the TCP connection. They suspect the SDK does not notice EOF promptly. Observed on go1.22, redwood-sdk-go v0.9.1. Impact: goroutines leak and request cancellation fails.

title: Keystore rollover triggered SIGABRT chain in runtime leading to elevated 5xx rate in prod us-east
preview:
Issue summary:
During a scheduled keystore rotation in prod us-east on 2026-03-13 15:40 UTC, we observed a sharp increase in 5xx responses from the API gateway. Investigation indicates multiple downstream serving runtime processes terminated with SIGABRT shortly after the keystore swap, causing elevated error rate and client request failures.

Impact:
- ~12% error rate increase across affected routes for ~18 minutes.
- Several enterprise customers (including AcmeCloud) reported failed streaming and generation requests.
- Automated retries caused additional load and exacerbated upstream failures.

Environment:
- prod-us-east (us-east-1)
- API Gateway version: apigw-v2.14.1
- Serving runtime: runtime-v1.86 (NVIDIA accel nodes)
- Keystore service: vault-rotator-v0.9

Observed behavior:
- Keystore rotation completed at 15:40 UTC. 15:41:10 UTC first SIGABRT observed in runtime-worker-12. Within 2 minutes 8 runtime workers crashed in rapid succession. API Gateway began returning 502/503s and 504s for in-flight requests.
- Affected routes: /v1/generate, /v1/streams. Non-streaming small-embed endpoints saw degradation but less severe.

Immediate mitigation applied:
- Rolled back keystore to previous version (15:48 UTC) and paused automated rotations.
- Temporarily increased graceful restart window on serving runtime to avoid simultaneous process exit.
- Put affected customers on dedicated fallback routes to minimize impact while we investigate.
text:

Investigation indicates multiple downstream serving runtime processes terminated with SIGABRT shortly after the keystore swap, causing elevated error rate and client request failures.

Immediate mitigation applied:
- Rolled back keystore to previous version (15:48 UTC) and paused automated rotations.

- Put affected customers on dedicated fallback routes to minimize impact while we investigate.

title: Dedicated pool throughput suppression caused by silent-tenant heartbeat damping
preview:
Issue summary: BrightScribe reported a sustained P99 throughput degradation for their dedicated pool during midday on 2026-03-12. Impact: degraded request throughput and elevated queuing leading to increased tail latency for inference requests. Root behaviour appears to be the autoscaler suppressing scale-ups due to misleading heartbeat-derived utilization signals from one or more silent tenants.

Impact details:
- Customer-facing: increased request backpressure, timeouts for a subset of long-lived streams, and ~25% drop in p50 throughput for routed model 'redwood/longform-16k'.
- Internal: autoscaler did not trigger expected burst additions for ~18 minutes despite GPU queue depth > 90% for several allocation groups.

Environment: Dedicated capacity, reserved pool id rp-47, us-east, auto-scale policy 'midday-burst' with predictive window enabled.

Observed by: BrightScribe Support and console alerting (route p99 queue depth alert).
text:

Impact: degraded request throughput and elevated queuing leading to increased tail latency for inference requests. Root behaviour appears to be the autoscaler suppressing scale-ups due to misleading heartbeat-derived utilization signals from one or more silent tenants.

Impact details:

- Internal: autoscaler did not trigger expected burst additions for ~18 minutes despite GPU queue depth > 90% for several allocation groups.

Observed by: BrightScribe Support and console alerting (route p99 queue depth alert).

title: Intermittent assume-role failures due to mismatched session tag attribute encoding between GCP WIF and AWS STS
preview: Summary: Jobs that bootstrap dedicated GPU provisioners via the auth-proxy intermittently fail to assume the target AWS role. Errors show AccessDenied: Not authorized to perform sts:AssumeRole or downstream permission checks fail when role session tags parsed by the provisioner are missing expected attributes. This started after last week's auth-proxy update that changed WIF-to-STS attribute normalization. Impact: Several Dedicated onboarding runs and internal automated repro jobs failed, blocking a small number of customer onboarding tasks and delaying a production scale test. Intermittent: ~10% of assume-role attempts in the last 48h failed.
text: Summary: Jobs that bootstrap dedicated GPU provisioners via the auth-proxy intermittently fail to assume the target AWS role. Errors show AccessDenied: Not authorized to perform sts:AssumeRole or downstream permission checks fail when role session tags parsed by the provisioner are missing expected attributes. This started after last week's auth-proxy update that changed WIF-to-STS attribute normalization. Impact: Several Dedicated onboarding runs and internal automated repro jobs failed, blocking a small number of customer onboarding tasks and delaying a production scale test. Intermittent: ~10% of assume-role attempts in the last 48h failed.

title: Delayed Okta group propagation + identity-proxy session cache mismatch causing 403 on feature-flag console role swap
preview: Users who are moved between Okta groups (role change) intermittently receive 403 when switching roles in the feature-flag console. The UI shows a successful role swap, but subsequent API calls return 403 until the user logs out or their session is refreshed. Issue appears tied to delayed group propagation from Okta and stale claim caching in identity-proxy.
text: Users who are moved between Okta groups (role change) intermittently receive 403 when switching roles in the feature-flag console. The UI shows a successful role swap, but subsequent API calls return 403 until the user logs out or their session is refreshed. Issue appears tied to delayed group propagation from Okta and stale claim caching in identity-proxy.

title: Source-port preservation mismatch across peered transit gateway causing intermittent gRPC healthcheck collisions
preview:
Issue summary: Customer running Redwood Private in a VPC peered via a Transit Gateway (TGW) across two accounts sees intermittent 5xx errors and flapping serving nodes. Problem appears correlated to gRPC health checks and long-lived streaming connections.

Impact: Several serving nodes report transient healthcheck failures and gRPC stream resets causing ~2-5% increase in 5xxs for production routes during peak. Customer reports visible user-facing latency spikes and a small number of failed streaming responses.

Environment: Private deployment using TGW peering between customer-network-account (containing egress NAT appliances) and redwood-hosting-account. NAT appliances are third-party SD-WAN appliances managed by the customer; AWS TGW route propagation enabled. Serving nodes behind an internal NLB (target group: HTTP/2 over TLS).

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start a steady stream of long-lived gRPC requests from the customer-side agent to Redwood serving pool (prod).
2) Simulate concurrent short TCP connections (healthchecks) originating from same NAT appliance.
3) Observe occasional gRPC stream resets and healthcheck failures in Redwood node logs.

Observed behavior:
- Serving runtime logs show healthcheck connections arriving that appear to reuse the same 4-tuple (srcIP:srcPort -> dstIP:dstPort) mapping as active streams for short intervals.
- gRPC multiplexed streams occasionally receive frames that the runtime treats as belonging to an already-closed stream, producing immediate RST-like resets and 502/503 to the client.
- Packet captures (customer-provided) show source port translation differing between the local AZ egress path and the TGW-peered path — ports are preserved on the local NAT but remapped when traversing the TGW boundary.

Expected behavior: Source port mapping should be stable or unique per connection so connection tracking on the serving nodes doesn't conflate independent flows. Healthchecks should not collide with long-lived application streams.

Initial logs / attachments: Customer uploaded a 30s pcap of reproduced failure (attached) and our initial serving node logs (serving-runtime.log). Relevant snippets of the serving runtime show repeated "unexpected stream id" followed by connection terminate.

Workarounds attempted by customer:
- Disabled TCP keepalive consolidation on the SD-WAN (no change).
- Reduced healthcheck frequency to 5s (reduced occurrence but did not eliminate).
- Temporarily placed healthchecks on a separate NLB listener port (mitigates immediately but not viable long-term).

Hypothesis: The SD-WAN NAT appliance preserves source ports locally, but the TGW/peering or an intermediate SNAT (or port-allocation policy on the transit device) rewrites ports when forwarding between VPCs/accounts. That causes two distinct client connections to appear to the backend as having identical 4-tuple for brief windows, which the serving runtime's connection demux code then misattributes, leading to stream collisions under HTTP/2 multiplexing.

Next steps (planned):
- SRE (assigned) to capture server-side tcpdump from affected serving node during repro window.
- Ask customer to provide a short-lived nat-trace from the SD-WAN/TGW showing port mapping before/after transit.
- Open a support ticket with the customer's transit vendor and AWS TGW support if TGW observed behavior is confirmed.
- Evaluate a mitigation in the runtime to tolerate transient 4-tuple collisions (short-term guard: per-connection TLS session binding + stricter TLS session id checks).

Runbooks / links: Slack thread #support-private (link: https://redwood.slack.com/archives/C01234567), customer pcap (attached in ticket), internal quick-runbook (https://confluence.redwood.ai/runbooks/tgw-nat-port-collisions).

title: Negative provisional balance and missing reserved credits after dedicated failover and cross-region proration
preview:
Issue summary: Customer BrightArc Health reports a negative provisional balance on their monthly bill after an automated dedicated failover between us-west and us-east. Reserved capacity credits that should have applied to the failover window are missing, and the invoice shows a -$1,240 provisional line item that the customer can't reconcile.

Impact: Customer-facing invoice variance for a healthcare customer (enterprise, dedicated) with potential chargeback to internal cost center. Customer is asking for an expedited reconciliation and provisional credit while we investigate.

Environment: Dedicated pool assigned to BrightArc Health, primary region us-west, failover triggered to us-east during scheduled maintenance window (2026-03-06 23:50 UTC). Billing window is hourly-based with proration.

Expected behavior: Reserved-hours proration should allocate hours across regions without creating negative provisional balances. Postbacks from edge proxies should be attributed to the correct hourly bucket and carry reserved credits applied at the reserved rate.

Actual behavior: After failover the hourly postback reordering and a cross-region proration step appear to have double-counted a partial refund and removed the reserved-hour credit for the failover window, resulting in a negative provisional balance.

Customer request: Immediate provisional credit so final invoice does not block autopay and a postmortem explaining root cause and guardrails put in place.
text:
Issue summary: Customer BrightArc Health reports a negative provisional balance on their monthly bill after an automated dedicated failover between us-west and us-east. Reserved capacity credits that should have applied to the failover window are missing, and the invoice shows a -$1,240 provisional line item that the customer can't reconcile.

Environment: Dedicated pool assigned to BrightArc Health, primary region us-west, failover triggered to us-east during scheduled maintenance window (2026-03-06 23:50 UTC). Billing window is hourly-based with proration.

Actual behavior: After failover the hourly postback reordering and a cross-region proration step appear to have double-counted a partial refund and removed the reserved-hour credit for the failover window, resulting in a negative provisional balance.

Customer request: Immediate provisional credit so final invoice does not block autopay and a postmortem explaining root cause and guardrails put in place.

title: Org policy deny on aws:TagCondition blocks cross-account ML deploys and GCP service-account impersonation
preview: Summary: Multiple ML deployment jobs for Dedicated customers failed when the provisioning pipeline attempted to assume a cross-account role and then impersonate a GCP service account. Impact: 6 deployment jobs for two enterprise tenants failed intermittently between 2026-03-01 02:10 UTC and 2026-03-01 03:45 UTC. Cause hypothesis: an AWS Organization Service Control Policy (SCP) with TagCondition-based denies blocks the sts:TagSession or ec2:RunInstances call used by the provisioner, causing the assume-role chain to fail and the downstream GCP impersonation to abort. This prevents creation of ephemeral credentials and blocks the dedicated capacity provisioning flow. User-visible impact: deployment retries surface 403/AccessDenied errors in the console and CI pipelines report "permission denied". Business impact: Dedicated customers in enterprise tier experienced delayed rollouts; no data exposure detected.
text: Summary: Multiple ML deployment jobs for Dedicated customers failed when the provisioning pipeline attempted to assume a cross-account role and then impersonate a GCP service account. Impact: 6 deployment jobs for two enterprise tenants failed intermittently between 2026-03-01 02:10 UTC and 2026-03-01 03:45 UTC. Cause hypothesis: an AWS Organization Service Control Policy (SCP) with TagCondition-based denies blocks the sts:TagSession or ec2:RunInstances call used by the provisioner, causing the assume-role chain to fail and the downstream GCP impersonation to abort. This prevents creation of ephemeral credentials and blocks the dedicated capacity provisioning flow. User-visible impact: deployment retries surface 403/AccessDenied errors in the console and CI pipelines report "permission denied". Business impact: Dedicated customers in enterprise tier experienced delayed rollouts; no data exposure detected.

title: warmup-lag-gpu-starvation-on-bursty-short-streams-causing-gradual-p99-drift
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers using Dedicated pool for FathomAI observed a gradual increase in p99 latency and a drop in sustained throughput during midday bursts. Symptoms correlate with autoscaler scale-up events where new GPU attachments were delayed and short-lived streams queued behind long-lived prefetch operations.

Impact:
- Customer FathomAI reported elevated p99 from ~450ms to 1.6s across batching routes during burst windows.
- Requests were queued for up to 20s before allocation in worst cases; observed error rate increased to 0.6% (mostly 502/504).
- Affects dedicated pool customers in prod us-east; FathomAI revenue-impacting SLA window.

Environment:
- Dedicated capacity (reserved pool) in us-east-1
- Model: llama-2-13b and redwood-tuned/13b-v1
- Routes: /v1/chat/stream and /v1/generate/batched (low-latency, many short streams)

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Ramp 50-150 simultaneous clients sending short streams (avg 40 tokens) over a 30s burst window.
2) Autoscaler triggers predictive scale-up for reserved pool.
3) New GPU attachments begin warmup: KV-cache prefetch + checkpoint validation + sidecar health checks.
4) Short streams are scheduled but stall behind long prefetch operations and slot reservation logic.
5) P99 and queue depth rise for 5-25 minutes until warmup completes or fallback to lower-tier models happens.

Observed behavior:
- Allocation delay (time-to-attach) metric spiked from median 2s to median 12s during events.
- Prefetch duration histogram shows a long tail: 95th percentile ~3.8s, 99th ~11.4s.
- Slot fragmentation: many GPUs show <20% util while request queue remains non-empty.
- Evidence of \"prefetch stampede\" where cache preload operations serialized on the same GPU process and blocked short, latency-sensitive streams.

Expected behavior:
- Predictive autoscale should pre-warm GPUs without blocking low-latency short streams; short streams should either be routed to prewarmed fallback capacity or served on warm GPUs with KV-cache hit.

Logs / metrics (snippets):
- autoscaler.event: {\"event\":\"scale_up_start\",\"pool\":\"dedicated-us-east-1\",\"delta\":4,\"timestamp\":\"2026-03-01T12:03:07Z\"}
- alloc.attach.lat_ms: p50=2100, p95=3800, p99=11400
- serving.prefetch.duration_ms: p50=420, p95=3800, p99=11400
- scheduler.queue.depth: mean=6, p95=34, p99=78
- error.rate: 0.006 (502/504 transient)

Notes on customer report:
- Customer observed degraded experience for 12:03–12:28 UTC; they rerouted to hosted API temporarily.
- Customer flagged as enterprise support; high business impact.

Investigation so far:
- Support (Aisha) validated customer logs and supplied request IDs and timestamps.
- SRE (Ben) reproduced queuing pattern in a staging reservation using synthetic short streams and verified prefetch long tail.
- Hypothesis: The warmup sequence (checkpoint verification -> kv prefetch -> sidecar readiness) currently acquires a GPU-local lock that serializes prefetches and blocks the allocation queue for other incoming short-stream requests. The autoscaler assumes warmup is background; in practice warmup is foreground and can cause slot hold.
- Observed correlation between sidecar restart events and increased attach latency; sidecar health probe frequency causes transient attachment stalls.

Next steps / actions:
- Short-term: Implement routing policy to prefer existing warm GPUs for short streams and queue long prefetch operations asynchronously. Temporary policy patch deployed to the route-level router to prefer fallback warm pool (rolled out 2026-03-02 03:20 UTC).
- Medium-term: SRE to update warmup workflow to async prefetch (decouple checkpoint validation from slot reservation) and add per-GPU warmup concurrency of N=3. Patch in review: PR #2487.
- Long-term: Autoscaler to incorporate warmup cost into predictive sizing and maintain a small warm buffer for low-latency routes.

Workaround implemented:
- Route-level fallback enabled: short-stream routes are now routed to a kept-warm cohort of GPUs or to a lower-capacity warm fallback cluster. This reduced measured attach latency p99 from 11.4s to 2.1s in controlled tests.

Resolution notes (provisional):
- Temporary routing policy reduced customer impact; FathomAI reports resumed normal UX after rerouting.
- Code change to decouple prefetch (PR #2487) is in review and staged for canary next week.
- Postmortem and follow-up action items created (see linked Confluence).
text:
Issue summary:
Customers using Dedicated pool for FathomAI observed a gradual increase in p99 latency and a drop in sustained throughput during midday bursts. Symptoms correlate with autoscaler scale-up events where new GPU attachments were delayed and short-lived streams queued behind long-lived prefetch operations.

Impact:
- Customer FathomAI reported elevated p99 from ~450ms to 1.6s across batching routes during burst windows.
- Requests were queued for up to 20s before allocation in worst cases; observed error rate increased to 0.6% (mostly 502/504).
- Affects dedicated pool customers in prod us-east; FathomAI revenue-impacting SLA window.

Environment:
- Dedicated capacity (reserved pool) in us-east-1
- Model: llama-2-13b and redwood-tuned/13b-v1
- Routes: /v1/chat/stream and /v1/generate/batched (low-latency, many short streams)

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Ramp 50-150 simultaneous clients sending short streams (avg 40 tokens) over a 30s burst window.
2) Autoscaler triggers predictive scale-up for reserved pool.
3) New GPU attachments begin warmup: KV-cache prefetch + checkpoint validation + sidecar health checks.
4) Short streams are scheduled but stall behind long prefetch operations and slot reservation logic.
5) P99 and queue depth rise for 5-25 minutes until warmup completes or fallback to lower-tier models happens.

Observed behavior:
- Allocation delay (time-to-attach) metric spiked from median 2s to median 12s during events.
- Prefetch duration histogram shows a long tail: 95th percentile ~3.8s, 99th ~11.4s.
- Slot fragmentation: many GPUs show <20% util while request queue remains non-empty.
- Evidence of \"prefetch stampede\" where cache preload operations serialized on the same GPU process and blocked short, latency-sensitive streams.

Expected behavior:
- Predictive autoscale should pre-warm GPUs without blocking low-latency short streams; short streams should either be routed to prewarmed fallback capacity or served on warm GPUs with KV-cache hit.

Logs / metrics (snippets):
- autoscaler.event: {\"event\":\"scale_up_start\",\"pool\":\"dedicated-us-east-1\",\"delta\":4,\"timestamp\":\"2026-03-01T12:03:07Z\"}
- alloc.attach.lat_ms: p50=2100, p95=3800, p99=11400
- serving.prefetch.duration_ms: p50=420, p95=3800, p99=11400
- scheduler.queue.depth: mean=6, p95=34, p99=78
- error.rate: 0.006 (502/504 transient)

Notes on customer report:
- Customer observed degraded experience for 12:03–12:28 UTC; they rerouted to hosted API temporarily.
- Customer flagged as enterprise support; high business impact.

Investigation so far:
- Support (Aisha) validated customer logs and supplied request IDs and timestamps.
- SRE (Ben) reproduced queuing pattern in a staging reservation using synthetic short streams and verified prefetch long tail.
- Hypothesis: The warmup sequence (checkpoint verification -> kv prefetch -> sidecar readiness) currently acquires a GPU-local lock that serializes prefetches and blocks the allocation queue for other incoming short-stream requests. The autoscaler assumes warmup is background; in practice warmup is foreground and can cause slot hold.
- Observed correlation between sidecar restart events and increased attach latency; sidecar health probe frequency causes transient attachment stalls.

Next steps / actions:
- Short-term: Implement routing policy to prefer existing warm GPUs for short streams and queue long prefetch operations asynchronously. Temporary policy patch deployed to the route-level router to prefer fallback warm pool (rolled out 2026-03-02 03:20 UTC).
- Medium-term: SRE to update warmup workflow to async prefetch (decouple checkpoint validation from slot reservation) and add per-GPU warmup concurrency of N=3. Patch in review: PR #2487.
- Long-term: Autoscaler to incorporate warmup cost into predictive sizing and maintain a small warm buffer for low-latency routes.

Workaround implemented:
- Route-level fallback enabled: short-stream routes are now routed to a kept-warm cohort of GPUs or to a lower-capacity warm fallback cluster. This reduced measured attach latency p99 from 11.4s to 2.1s in controlled tests.

Resolution notes (provisional):
- Temporary routing policy reduced customer impact; FathomAI reports resumed normal UX after rerouting.
- Code change to decouple prefetch (PR #2487) is in review and staged for canary next week.
- Postmortem and follow-up action items created (see linked Confluence).

title: VPCE ECMP hash change during rebalancing causing stale TLS session association and control-plane retries
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer NovaDocs reported intermittent control-plane auth retries and streaming stalls for private deployment behind a VPCE. Symptoms began after a scheduled network rebase in us-east-1.

Impact:
- A subset (~12%) of serving nodes in the cluster experienced higher TLS handshake latencies (50-500ms increase) and occasional 5xx control-plane retries.
- Affects production inference for models pinned to redwood-2-small; customer observed failed model pull retries and transient warmup failures impacting tail latency for user requests.

Environment:
- Private deployment (VPCE endpoint + Transit VPC) in us-east-1
- VPCE configured with ECMP to two transit appliances (managed by customer)
- Serving nodes use long-lived mutual-TLS connections to control-plane and artifact stores

Timeline / Observed events:
- 2026-03-01 02:10 UTC: Customer scheduled rebase for transit appliance pair (documented in their change window).
- 2026-03-01 02:12 UTC: Support received first alert for increased TLS handshake latency on control-plane metrics.
- 2026-03-01 02:16 UTC: Customer reported intermittent model pull failures and elevated retry counts.
- 2026-03-01 02:30-03:00 UTC: Spikes persisted then partially subsided; periodic spike pattern correlated with VPCE path rebalancing events.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1. Trigger ECMP hash change / reflow on transit appliances (customer performed controlled rebase).
2. Observe subset of nodes see TLS handshake re-negotiation and increase in session resume failures.
3. Control-plane auth requests from affected nodes show additional retry attempts and 504/502s reported by the SDK.

Logs / Key artifacts:
- Control-plane auth logs: repeated TLS session mismatches containing "session-id mismatch" and differing client IPs for the same TLS ticket.
- Flow logs (customer-provided): show inbound VPCE traffic from two different transit IPs during a single session lifetime.
- Serving node metrics: spike in handshake RT and KV cache warmup misses.
- Packet capture (limited): shows SYN/SYN-ACK flow via different ingress paths; server sees recreated connection  with re-used TLS session ticket but different 5-tuple.

Initial hypothesis:
ECMP path rebalancing changed the 5-tuple (source port or source IP) observed by the control-plane / artifact endpoints mid-session. The control-plane's TLS ticket/session resume validation is tied to the observed client fingerprint (5-tuple plus TLS session parameters) in the customer's environment and the VPCE path change caused resumed sessions to be rejected or create a new association. This leads to extra TLS handshakes, increased latency, and occasionally failed artifact downloads when upstream connection pools hit retry thresholds.

Investigation notes:
- Reproduced correlation across multiple nodes: affected connections show path change at reflow times reported by customer's transit appliances.
- Confirmed VPCE NAT behaviour: customer uses SNAT on the transit appliances that can rotate ephemeral source ports and occasionally change egress IP within a short window during rebind operations.
- Control-plane observed "session-binding" enforcement: our LB rejects resumed session tickets if client tuple differs beyond allowed variance.
- Related config: customer has aggressive ephemeral port reuse and asymmetric ECMP hashing between the two transit devices.

Temporary mitigation / recommended workaround (customer-adoptable immediately):
1. Configure transit appliances to preserve source IP/port affinity across ECMP rebalances (session stickiness) during the change window.
2. Increase TLS session ticket acceptance tolerance on the customer-side LB (if possible) to allow resume across minor tuple changes.
3. Stagger rebalances to avoid flipping the egress path for many long-lived connections simultaneously.

Longer-term actions / Next steps owned by Redwood and Customer:
- SRE: add heuristic detection to flag VPCE path reflows that correlate with handshake latency spikes (tracking ticket: INT-1309).
- Product/Routing: evaluate relaxing session-binding rules for control-plane endpoints for private deployments behind VPCEs where ECMP path churn is observed.
- Customer: update transit appliance ECMP hashing policy to include stable flow hashing keys (src-ip, dst-ip, dst-port) and disable ephemeral-port rotation during maintenance windows.
- Joint postmortem to be scheduled after stabilization with proposed config changes.

References:
- Slack thread: #support-private (link: https://slack.redwood.com/archives/C0SUPPORT/p164).
- Packet capture and flow logs attached to ticket (customer-supplied).

title: Streaming intermittently stalls for Northwind Robotics (no [DONE], client waits indefinitely)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer sees streaming responses stop after some tokens. Connection remains open; no terminal event.

Impact:
- Their in-product assistant gets stuck; they cancel and retry.

Environment:
- Hosted API in eu-west region.
- Using server-sent events over HTTPS via corporate proxy.

Triage notes:
- Metrics show elevated open connections with low egress bytes.
- Customer proxy may buffer small chunks; they reported it improves when disabling proxy or increasing chunk size.

Workaround shared:
- Disable HTTP buffering on proxy (nginx: proxy_buffering off), or use websocket transport (if available), or set client read timeout + retry.

Resolution:
- Not fully reproducible internally; linked to known issue page and provided proxy guidance. Marking closed once customer confirmed workaround reduced occurrences.
text:

- Customer sees streaming responses stop after some tokens. Connection remains open; no terminal event.

Resolution:
- Not fully reproducible internally; linked to known issue page and provided proxy guidance. Marking closed once customer confirmed workaround reduced occurrences.

title: Console onboarding nudge intermittently blocks API key creation (Create API key button unclickable)
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports they cannot create an API key in the Console. The "Create API key" button appears, but is not clickable / click does nothing.

Impact:
- Blocks activation milestone (API key creation → first request).
- Customer unable to complete onboarding and send first API call.
- Support volume risk during lifecycle growth experiments rollout (console nudges).

Customer context:
- Company: DevFlow AI
- Plan: self-serve Hosted API
- User report: "The onboarding checklist is telling me to create a key, but I can't click the button. It looks like something is on top of it."

Environment:
- Console: app.redwoodinference.com
- Observed in: prod (us-east)
- Browser: Chrome 121 / macOS (also reproduced on Arc)
- Feature flags: lifecycle growth console nudges enabled for cohort (holdout = false)

Steps to reproduce (internal repro):
1) Use a user in cohort with console onboarding nudges enabled.
2) Navigate to Console → Settings → API Keys.
3) If the "Next best action" nudge state is "create_api_key" and the nudge is rendered as a sticky banner, attempt to click "Create API key".
4) Result: button is visually enabled but click does not fire; cursor indicates underlying element is blocked.

Expected:
User can always create an API key. Nudges must not intercept pointer events on primary onboarding CTAs.

Actual:
In some states, the nudge container overlays the API key creation header/CTA region. Because the nudge wrapper has an incorrect z-index/pointer-events configuration, it captures clicks (and for some window sizes, overlaps the CTA entirely).

Notes / suspected cause:
- Regression likely introduced with the reusable nudge component rollout.
- Seems sensitive to viewport height and whether the checklist is expanded.
- Potential state mismatch: after dismissal, the nudge component re-renders on API Keys route with stale step state and keeps overlay position.

Workaround (until fix):
- Reduce zoom to 90% or collapse the checklist panel (if visible) and hard refresh.
- Alternatively create API key via the Redwood CLI (shared with customer) while we hotfix Console.

Requested outcome:
Hotfix in Console to ensure onboarding nudges never block API key creation flow. Validate across common viewports and keep behind the lifecycle-growth experiment flag.

title: Validate/adjust DKIM/SPF/DMARC + SendGrid authenticated domain config for scaling lifecycle email volume
preview:
Request
- We are ramping volume for the Hosted API onboarding email v2 as part of the Lifecycle Growth Experiments sprint.
- Need confirmation that Redwood’s sending domain authentication is correctly set up and aligned with our current provider (SendGrid), and that SPF/DKIM/DMARC are passing with alignment for the From domain.

Background / impact
- We saw an increase in “didn’t receive onboarding email” reports and deliverability variability as we started ramping (esp. Gmail).
- Risk: scaling volume without correct authentication will amplify spam folder placement and/or bounces, and invalidate experiment readouts.

Scope
1) DNS: verify SPF record(s) for the From domain used by lifecycle emails.
2) DNS: verify DKIM keys (CNAMEs) for SendGrid authenticated domain are present and matching SendGrid UI.
3) DNS: verify DMARC exists and is reasonable for our current maturity (monitor first, then tighten).
4) SendGrid: verify authenticated domain + custom return-path/bounce domain configured, and that the From address matches the authenticated domain.

What I need from Internal Support / Marketing Ops
- Confirm current “From” domain and SendGrid authenticated domain.
- Provide exact DNS records required (host/name + type + value) and confirm after propagation.
- Provide quick validation output (header authentication results) for a test message.

Notes
- We’ll keep ramp conservative until authentication is confirmed.
- If DMARC is missing or misconfigured, propose minimal safe config (p=none + rua) to start.

References
- Related customer ticket volume: SUP-24871 (Not receiving onboarding emails)
- Ongoing deliverability thread with vendor support referenced in sprint notes (SendGrid investigation)

Acceptance criteria
- DKIM: PASS for lifecycle emails with alignment to the From domain.
- SPF: PASS with alignment (or documented rationale if using subdomain/return-path alignment).
- DMARC: PASS (at least p=none) with reporting enabled.
- Document final records + where to monitor (SendGrid, Postmaster Tools).

title: Northwind AI reports usage token totals higher than expected (streaming + retries)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports Redwood Usage dashboard + invoice preview shows ~18-25% more tokens than their internal estimates for same traffic.

Impact:
- Finance reconciliation blocked; escalation risk.

Context:
- Customer uses streaming. Client timeout 15s and retries once on timeout.
- Customer assumption: timed-out attempt should not be billed if they did not receive full response.

Initial hypothesis:
- Usage is counted per admitted attempt (retries are separate).
- Prompt tokens counted once admitted even if client cancels stream.
- Need to confirm there is no double-counting of completion tokens on partial streams.

Requested from customer:
- Sample request IDs + timeframe
- Retry/cancel rate metrics

Links:
- Slack: #support thread_ts=1739310605
- Slack: #eng-platform thread_ts=1739386202
- Gmail thread: 20250212-northwind-usage-token-count-mismatch-escalation (Vanessa Ortiz)
